This subsection builds the sole remaining ingredient of the gap1 keystone descent
\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}: the geometric \emph{producer}
\cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen} that manufactures, for a quasi-coherent \(M\) on
\(\Spec R\) and a basic open \(D(s)\) inside a quasi-coherence cover member, the per-cover-element
localization datum \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) the basic-open cover form
\cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover} consumes. The construction transports the
\(R_s\)-level localization living on the affine slice \(D(s) \cong \Spec R_s\) back to an
\(R\)-level statement through the combiner \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport}; the four
sub-lemmas below supply the geometric inputs (an iterated-pullback \(\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) iso, the
image/range computations, the \(\top\)-level semilinearity, and the scalar-tower instances).
input.new_string:
This subsection builds the geometric input of the gap1 keystone descent
\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}: the geometric \emph{producer}
\cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen} that manufactures, for a quasi-coherent \(M\) on
\(\Spec R\) and a basic open \(D(s)\) inside a quasi-coherence cover member, the per-cover-element
localization datum \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) the basic-open cover form
\cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover} consumes. The construction transports the
\(R_s\)-level localization living on the affine slice \(D(s) \cong \Spec R_s\) back to an
\(R\)-level statement through the combiner \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport}; the
geometric inputs --- the iterated-pullback tilde--\(\Gamma\) isomorphism, the identification of the
immersion's image opens, and the semilinear section comparisons --- enter in the proof of the
producer.

\begin{lemma}[Localization transport across a semilinear section comparison]
  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_powers_transport}
  \leanok
  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(A\) an \(R\)-algebra, \(S\) a commutative ring, and
  \(\sigma : S \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } A\) a ring isomorphism; let \(f \in R\) and
  \(f' \in S\) with \(\sigma(f')\) equal to the image of \(f\) in \(A\). Let
  \(g : M_1 \to M_2\) be an \(S\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_2\) as the localization of
  \(M_1\) at the powers of \(f'\); let \(N_1, N_2\) be \(A\)-modules, regarded also as
  \(R\)-modules through \(R \to A\); let \(e_1 : M_1 \to N_1\) and \(e_2 : M_2 \to N_2\)
  be additive isomorphisms that are \(\sigma\)-semilinear, i.e.
  \(e_i(a \cdot x) = \sigma(a) \cdot e_i(x)\) for \(a \in S\); and let
  \(h : N_1 \to N_2\) be an \(A\)-linear map intertwining \(g\), i.e.
  \(h \circ e_1 = e_2 \circ g\). Then \(h\), regarded as an \(R\)-linear map, exhibits
  \(N_2\) as the localization of \(N_1\) at the powers of \(f\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \leanok
  We check the three localization conditions in two stages.

  \emph{Stage I: crossing \(\sigma\).} The map \(h\) is a localization at the powers of
  \(\sigma(f')\) over \(A\).
  \emph{Units:} for \(k \ge 0\), the semilinearity of \(e_2\) gives that the action of
  \(\sigma(f')^k = \sigma(f'^k)\) on \(N_2\) equals
  \(e_2 \circ (\text{action of } f'^k \text{ on } M_2) \circ e_2^{-1}\), a composite of
  bijections because \(f'^k\) acts invertibly on the localization \(M_2\).
  \emph{Surjectivity:} given \(y \in N_2\), choose \(x \in M_1\) and \(k\) with
  \(f'^k \cdot e_2^{-1}(y) = g(x)\); applying \(e_2\) and semilinearity,
  \(\sigma(f')^k \cdot y = e_2(g(x)) = h(e_1(x))\).
  \emph{Uniqueness:} if \(h(y_1) = h(y_2)\) then, by the intertwining relation,
  \(e_2\bigl(g(e_1^{-1} y_1)\bigr) = e_2\bigl(g(e_1^{-1} y_2)\bigr)\), so
  \(g(e_1^{-1} y_1) = g(e_1^{-1} y_2)\); hence
  \(f'^k \cdot e_1^{-1} y_1 = f'^k \cdot e_1^{-1} y_2\) for some \(k\), and applying
  \(e_1\), \(\sigma(f')^k \cdot y_1 = \sigma(f')^k \cdot y_2\).

  \emph{Stage II: descending to \(R\).} By hypothesis \(\sigma(f')\) is the image of
  \(f\) under \(R \to A\), and by compatibility of the two module structures the action
  of \(f^k\) through \(R\) coincides with the action of the image of \(f^k\) through
  \(A\), on \(N_1\) and on \(N_2\). Each of the three conditions at the powers of \(f\)
  over \(R\) is therefore literally the corresponding Stage-I condition at the powers of
  \(\sigma(f')\) over \(A\): the units condition transfers because the two actions
  coincide; surjectivity reads \(f^k \cdot y = \sigma(f')^k \cdot y = h(x)\); and
  uniqueness likewise.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Section-transport producer: basic-open localization inside a cover member
(gap1, Hfr)]
  \label{lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport,
    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}
  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) with quasi-coherence data (an open cover
  \(\{X_i\}\) with per-member presentations, as in
  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}), let \(f, s \in R\), and
  suppose \(D(s) \subseteq X_i\) for some \(i\). Then the section restriction
  \[
    \Gamma\bigl(M, D(s)\bigr) \longrightarrow \Gamma\bigl(M, D(f) \cap D(s)\bigr)
  \]
  exhibits the target as the localization of \(\Gamma(M, D(s))\) at the powers of \(f\),
  over \(R\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport,
    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}
  Let \(W := \iota_{X_i}^{-1}(D(s))\) be the affine open of the subscheme \(Z := X_i\)
  corresponding to \(D(s)\), put \(S := \Gamma(Z, W)\), and let
  \(j : \Spec S \to \Spec R\) be the composite open immersion
  \(\Spec S \cong W \hookrightarrow Z \hookrightarrow \Spec R\) (the affine
  identification of \(W\) followed by the two canonical immersions). Its image is
  \(D(s)\): the first leg is an isomorphism onto \(W\), and
  \(\iota_{X_i}(W) = X_i \cap D(s) = D(s)\).

  Write \(M' := j^{*} M\) (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}). The per-element
  transport \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent} gives invertibility of
  the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit for the iterated pullback of \(M\) along the three legs of
  \(j\); pullback along a composite agrees with the composite of the pullbacks up to
  canonical natural isomorphism, and invertibility of the counit is invariant under
  isomorphism of modules (the essential image of the tilde functor is closed under
  isomorphism). Hence the counit of \(M'\) is invertible, and by
  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}, for every \(f' \in S\) the
  restriction \(\Gamma(M', \top) \to \Gamma(M', D(f'))\) is the localization at the
  powers of \(f'\) over \(S\).

  Let \(\sigma : S \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Gamma(\Spec R, D(s))\) be the ring comparison
  of the open immersion \(j\) on top sections
  (\(S \cong \Gamma(\Spec S, \top) \cong \Gamma(\Spec R, j(\top))\), and
  \(j(\top) = D(s)\)), and choose \(f' := \sigma^{-1}\bigl(f|_{D(s)}\bigr)\). The image
  \(j\bigl(D(f')\bigr)\) is the locus of \(D(s)\) where \(\sigma(f') = f|_{D(s)}\) is
  invertible, that is, \(j\bigl(D(f')\bigr) = D(f) \cap D(s)\).

  Since \(j\) is an open immersion, applying the section functor to the identification of
  the pullback with the restriction (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib})
  gives additive isomorphisms
  \(e_1 : \Gamma(M', \top) \cong \Gamma\bigl(M, D(s)\bigr)\) and
  \(e_2 : \Gamma(M', D(f')) \cong \Gamma\bigl(M, D(f) \cap D(s)\bigr)\) which intertwine
  the two restriction maps (naturality of the identification in the open) and are
  \(\sigma\)-semilinear over the structure-sheaf comparisons: the action on the sections
  of the pullback is through the immersion's section-ring isomorphism.

  Finally apply the combiner \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport} with
  \(A := \Gamma(\Spec R, D(s))\) — an \(R\)-algebra through restriction of global
  sections — the isomorphism \(\sigma\), the elements \(f\) and \(f'\) (so that
  \(\sigma(f')\) is the image of \(f\) in \(A\)), the localization
  \(g := \bigl(\Gamma(M', \top) \to \Gamma(M', D(f'))\bigr)\), the comparisons
  \(e_1, e_2\), and \(h := \bigl(\Gamma(M, D(s)) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \cap D(s))\bigr)\):
  the conclusion is that \(h\) is the localization at the powers of \(f\) over \(R\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Section-localization descent for quasi-coherent modules (gap1 keystone)]
  \label{lem:section_localization_descent}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover,
    lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}
  For a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules \(M\) on \(\Spec R\) and \(f \in R\), the section
  restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the
  localization \(\Gamma(M, \top)[1/f]\) over \(R\). This is the statement of
  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} at the affine open \(\top\) of
  \(\Spec R\), proved here by finite-cover descent --- not through the affine
  equivalence of quasi-coherent modules with modules, which is instead \emph{derived}
  from this lemma in \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover,
    lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}
  Quasi-coherence provides a cover \(\{X_i\}\) of \(\Spec R\) with per-member
  presentations. Refine it to a finite basic-open cover: the basic opens form a basis of
  \(\Spec R\), so every point has a basic-open neighbourhood contained in some \(X_i\);
  quasi-compactness of \(\Spec R\) extracts a finite family \(t \subseteq R\) with each
  \(D(r)\), \(r \in t\), contained in some \(X_i\), and covering is equivalent to \(t\)
  generating the unit ideal. For every basic open \(D(u)\) contained in some \(D(r)\)
  with \(r \in t\) — hence contained in a cover member \(X_i\) — the producer
  \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen} supplies the localization datum at
  \(D(u)\). The basic-open cover form
  \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover} then yields the conclusion.
\end{proof}

input.new_string:
This subsection records the bridge \emph{section localization on every basic open}
\(\Rightarrow\) \emph{invertible tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit}: given that the section
restriction localizes on every basic open, the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit is an
isomorphism. Together with its converse
\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ} it assembles the equivalence
\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}, which is exactly what makes G1-core
(\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}) and gap1
(\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}) interderivable -- the route by which G1-core is obtained as
a corollary of gap1. The supporting Mathlib facts enter through the tilde--\(\Gamma\)
adjunction anchor (\cref{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}).

% --- Project-original G1-assemble nodes (Lean realizations) ---

\begin{lemma}[Basic-open section localization for a module with invertible
tilde--\texorpdfstring{\(\Gamma\)}{Gamma} counit]
  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: Stacks, Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-widetilde-pullback} and the remark
  % following it (restricting a tilde to a standard open D(f) yields the tilde of the
  % localized module; read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1242-L1274).
  \textit{Source: Stacks, Schemes, Lemma \texttt{widetilde-pullback} (sections of a
  tilde over a basic open are the localized module).}
  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) whose tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(M, \top)} \to M\) (\cref{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}) is an
  isomorphism. Then for every \(f \in R\) the section restriction
  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the localization of
  \(\Gamma(M, \top)\) at the powers of \(f\), over \(R\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}
  First let \(M = \widetilde{N}\) be a tilde. The unit
  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top)\) of the adjunction
  (\cref{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}) is an isomorphism, and the canonical map
  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the localized module
  \(N_f\) — the sections of a tilde over a basic open are the localization. The
  canonical map factors as the unit followed by the section restriction, so the
  restriction \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top) \to \Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) is the
  composite of the inverse unit isomorphism with a localization map, hence itself a
  localization at the powers of \(f\).

  For general \(M\), put \(N := \Gamma(M, \top)\). The counit isomorphism
  \(\widetilde{N} \cong M\) induces, naturally in the open, \(R\)-linear isomorphisms
  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top) \cong \Gamma(M, \top)\) and
  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f)) \cong \Gamma(M, D(f))\) intertwining the two restriction
  maps. Pre- and post-composing a localization map with \(R\)-linear isomorphisms
  compatible in this way preserves the localization property.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Characterization of the invertible
tilde--\texorpdfstring{\(\Gamma\)}{Gamma} counit by section localization]
  \label{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}
  For a sheaf of modules \(M\) on \(\Spec R\), the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(M, \top)} \to M\) is an isomorphism if and only if for every
  \(f \in R\) the section restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits
  the target as the localization of \(\Gamma(M, \top)\) at the powers of \(f\) over
  \(R\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}
  The forward direction is \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}.

  For the converse, write \(N := \Gamma(M, \top)\) and let
  \(\varepsilon : \widetilde{N} \to M\) be the counit. Fix \(f \in R\). The
  \(D(f)\)-component of \(\varepsilon\) is a map between two localizations of \(N\) at
  the powers of \(f\): the source \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) receives the canonical
  map from \(N\) (sections of a tilde over a basic open, as in the forward direction),
  and the target \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\) receives the restriction
  \(N = \Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\), a localization by hypothesis. The
  component intertwines these two maps out of \(N\): the counit composed with the
  canonical section map is the section restriction, by the compatibility of the counit
  with the adjunction unit (\cref{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}) and naturality.
  A map between two localizations of the same module at the same multiplicative set
  commuting with the two localization maps is the canonical isomorphism between them,
  hence bijective. Thus \(\varepsilon\) is an isomorphism on every basic open.

  Since the basic opens form a basis of \(\Spec R\), \(\varepsilon\) is an isomorphism
  on every stalk: injective, because a germ in the kernel is represented by a section
  over some basic neighbourhood killed by \(\varepsilon\) there, hence zero; surjective,
  because every germ of \(M\) is the germ of a section over some basic open, which is
  hit by the corresponding section of \(\widetilde{N}\). A morphism of sheaves that is
  an isomorphism on all stalks is an isomorphism, and an isomorphism of the underlying
  sheaves underlying a morphism of sheaves of modules is an isomorphism of sheaves of
  modules.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[A quasi-coherent module on an affine scheme is the tilde of its sections
(gap1, Stacks 01I8)]
  \label{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isQuasicoherent}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
  % SOURCE: Stacks, Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine} (read from
  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1278-L1340): a quasi-coherent module on Spec(R) is
  % isomorphic to the sheaf associated to the R-module of its global sections.
  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01I8}{01I8}
  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}).}
  For a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules \(M\) on \(\Spec R\), the tilde--\(\Gamma\)
  counit \(\widetilde{\Gamma(M, \top)} \to M\) is an isomorphism; equivalently, \(M\)
  lies in the essential image of the tilde functor, i.e. \(M\) is isomorphic to the
  tilde of an \(R\)-module.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
  By the keystone descent \cref{lem:section_localization_descent}, every basic-open
  section restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is the localization at the
  powers of \(f\). The reverse implication of
  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict} concludes.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Affine section localization for quasi-coherent modules (G1-core)]
  \label{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_isQuasicoherent}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
  For a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules \(M\) on \(\Spec R\) and \(f \in R\), the
  section restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the
  localization \(\Gamma(M, \top)[1/f]\) over \(R\). This is the statement of
  \cref{lem:section_localization_descent} in its clean named form — obtained here as a
  corollary of gap1 rather than by re-running the descent — and the affine instance
  (\(X = \Spec R\), \(U = \top\)) of the general-scheme keystone
  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \leanok
  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
  Gap1 (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}) makes the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit of
  \(M\) an isomorphism, and
  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ} applies.
\end{proof}

  have hv₀ : Function.Surjective v₀ :=

  have huv₀ : Function.Exact u₀ v₀ :=

      (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap S M₂) (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap S M₃)

  exact ModuleCat.free_shortExact hC

\textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, Theorem (Grothendieck), Theorem
  (Altman--Kleiman) (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.}
  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a projective morphism,
  \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). Then the
  functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) of \cref{def:quot_functor} is
  representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\),
  equipped with a universal quotient
  \[
    q^{\mathrm{univ}} :
    \pi^*_{\Quot} E \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}^{\mathrm{univ}}
    \quad \text{on } X \times_S \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S},
  \]
  flat over \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) with constant Hilbert polynomial
  \(\Phi\) on every fiber. The Hilbert scheme is the special case
  \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\): \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} = \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)
  represents the functor of \(T\)-flat closed subschemes
  \(Y \subset X_T\) with fiberwise Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\).
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: this proof is long (~5 named sub-sections in [Nitsure]

% project notation in the \begin{proof} block.

      and any \(T \to S\) and any \(T\)-flat quotient

      direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
      \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of ranks fixed by the

2861	  \(D(r) \subseteq X_i\) for some \(i\), and let \(W := \iota_{X_i}^{-1}(D(r))\) be the
2862	  corresponding affine open of the subscheme \(Z := X_i\). Then the restriction of \(M\)
2863	  to \(D(r)\) — the pullback of \(M\) to \(Z\), then to \(W\), transported across the
2864	  affine identification \(W \cong \Spec\Gamma(Z, W)\) — has invertible
2865	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit (\cref{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}); that is,
2866	  \(M|_{D(r)}\) is the tilde of its section module over
2867	  \(\Gamma(Z, W) \cong R_r\) (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}).
2868	\end{lemma}
2869	\begin{proof}
2870	  \leanok
2871	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
2872	    lem:modules_pullback_mathlib, lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}
2873	  \emph{Step 1 (geometrize the datum).} The quasi-coherence datum supplies a presentation
2874	  of the slice restriction \(M.\mathrm{over}\,X_i\). The slice-to-geometric equivalence
2875	  (\cref{def:over_restrict_equiv}) preserves colimits and carries the unit module to the
2876	  unit module, so the image of this presentation, transported along the isomorphism of
2877	  \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso}, is a presentation of the geometric pullback
2878	  \(N := \iota_{X_i}^{*} M\) on the scheme \(Z\).
2879	
2880	  \emph{Step 2 (restrict to \(W\)).} Restriction to the slice site over \(W\) again
2881	  preserves colimits and the unit, so the global presentation of \(N\) slices to a
2882	  presentation of \(N.\mathrm{over}\,W\); geometrizing across the slice-to-geometric
2883	  equivalence at \(W\) (\cref{def:over_restrict_equiv},
2884	  \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso}) yields a presentation of the geometric
2885	  restriction \(\iota_W^{*} N\) on the open subscheme \(W\).
2886	
2887	  \emph{Step 3 (affine identification).} \(W\) is affine: \(D(r)\) is a basic open of the
2888	  affine scheme \(\Spec R\), and its preimage under the open immersion \(\iota_{X_i}\) is
2889	  again affine because \(D(r)\) is contained in the immersion's range. Pull the
2890	  presentation back across the isomorphism \(W \cong \Spec\Gamma(Z, W)\): the pullback of
2891	  sheaves of modules along an isomorphism of schemes
2892	  (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}) preserves colimits, being a left adjoint, and
2893	  carries the unit module to the unit module, since the induced functor on opens sites is
2894	  an equivalence. This lands a global presentation of the transported module on the
2895	  affine scheme \(\Spec\Gamma(Z, W)\).
2896	
2897	  \emph{Step 4 (a presented module on \(\Spec S\) is a tilde).} Let \(P\) be a sheaf of
2898	  modules on \(\Spec S\) with a presentation
2899	  \(\mathcal{O}^{\oplus J} \to \mathcal{O}^{\oplus K} \to P \to 0\). The map of coproducts
2900	  of units corresponds under the tilde--\(\Gamma\) adjunction
2901	  (\cref{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}) to a map of free modules
2902	  \(S^{\oplus J} \to S^{\oplus K}\), because \(\mathcal{O}^{\oplus K}\) is the tilde of
2903	  \(S^{\oplus K}\) and the unit of the adjunction is an isomorphism. Since the tilde
2904	  functor preserves cokernels (it is a left adjoint), \(P\) is isomorphic to the tilde of
2905	  the cokernel of that module map. Finally, a module isomorphic to a tilde has invertible
2906	  counit: on a tilde \(\widetilde{N}\) the triangle identity of the adjunction shows the
2907	  counit is inverse to the tilde of the unit isomorphism \(N \cong
2908	  \Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top)\), and invertibility of the counit transports across any
2909	  isomorphism of modules by naturality. Applying this to the transported module of
2910	  Step~3 concludes.
2911	\end{proof}
2912	
2913	% --- (D) The section-localization descent: the gap1 keystone ---
2914	
2915	% NOTE: the descent is stated for an ARBITRARY sheaf of modules under an explicit
2916	% per-cover-element localization hypothesis; it deliberately does NOT route through the global
2917	% affine equivalence (which is gap1 itself). The sheaf-gluing engines (separatedness, unique
2918	% gluing over the finite cover) are steps inside the proofs below.
2919	
2920	\begin{lemma}[Section-localization descent from per-cover localization data (gap1, D,
2921	cover form)]
2922	  \label{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover}

input.old_string:
The full algebraic statement — `A` a noetherian domain, `B` a *finite-type*
`A`-algebra, `M` a finite `B`-module ⟹ `∃ f ≠ 0` with `M_f` free over `A_f` —
is a deep theorem (prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
clearing denominators). Mathlib already supplies most of the dévissage
machinery (`IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime`,
`ModuleCat.free_shortExact`, `exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg`), but it does
**not** yet contain the polynomial-ring core (generic freeness for a finite
module over `A[X₁,…,X_d]`). See the file `task_results` handoff for the
precise decomposition of the remaining gap.

What we *can* land axiom-clean here is the **finite-module / finite-morphism
case**: when `M` is finite as an `A`-module (in particular when `B` is
module-finite over `A`), generic freeness follows directly from
`Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers` applied at the
generic point `Frac A`, where `M ⊗_A Frac A` is a finite vector space hence
free. This is a genuine special case of the algebraic generic-freeness
theorem (the case of a *finite* morphism `X → S`), and a reusable building
block for the dévissage's leaves. -/
input.new_string:
The full algebraic statement — `A` a noetherian domain, `B` a *finite-type*
`A`-algebra, `M` a finite `B`-module ⟹ `∃ f ≠ 0` with `M_f` free over `A_f` —
is a deep theorem (prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
clearing denominators). Mathlib does **not** contain it (checked against the
pinned revision; only the spreading-out lemma
`Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers` exists).

This section contains, axiom-clean: the **finite-module / finite-morphism
case** (`exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite`, via
`exists_free_localizedModule_powers` at the generic point), and — added in
run 0010 T12 r2 — the complete **dévissage layer** of Nitsure's proof:
`GenericallyFree` (the property), `free_localizedModule_powers_mul`
(localization transport `M_f` free ⟹ `M_{fg}` free),
`GenericallyFree.of_linearEquiv`, `genericallyFree_of_exact` (splicing
under short exact sequences, via `ModuleCat.free_shortExact`),
`genericallyFree_of_subsingleton`, `genericallyFree_of_torsion` (Nitsure's
`n = -1` base case), and the prime-filtration assembly
`genericFlatnessAlgebraic` via
`IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime`. The single
remaining `sorry` is the Noether-normalisation domain core
`genericallyFree_quotient_prime`. -/

2921	cover form)]
2922	  \label{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover}
2923	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent_of_cover}
2924	  \leanok
2925	  \uses{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
2926	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\), \(f \in R\), and
2927	  \(t \subseteq R\) a finite subset generating the unit ideal, so that the basic opens
2928	  \(\{D(r)\}_{r \in t}\) cover \(\Spec R\). Assume the localization datum: for every open
2929	  \(U\) contained in some \(D(r)\) with \(r \in t\), the section restriction
2930	  \[
2931	    \Gamma(M, U) \longrightarrow \Gamma\bigl(M, D(f) \cap U\bigr)
2932	  \]
2933	  exhibits the target as the localization of \(\Gamma(M, U)\) at the powers of \(f\),
2934	  over \(R\). Then the global section restriction
2935	  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\) as the
2936	  localization \(\Gamma(M, \top)[1/f]\) over \(R\).
2937	\end{lemma}
2938	\begin{proof}
2939	  \leanok
2940	  \uses{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
2941	  We verify the three conditions characterizing the localization map.
2942	
2943	  \emph{Units.} Every power of \(f\) acts invertibly on \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\): the action
2944	  of \(f\) on sections over \(D(f)\) is through the restriction of \(f\) to
2945	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R, D(f))\), which is a unit there (\(f\) is invertible on its own basic
2946	  open), and multiplication by a unit of the acting ring is bijective. This holds for an
2947	  arbitrary \(M\), without the covering hypothesis.
2948	
2949	  \emph{Torsion.} Let \(x \in \Gamma(M, \top)\) restrict to \(0\) on \(D(f)\); we find
2950	  \(n\) with \(f^n \cdot x = 0\) (applied to a difference \(x_1 - x_2\), this is the
2951	  uniqueness condition). For each \(r \in t\), the restriction \(x|_{D(r)}\) restricts to
2952	  \(0\) on \(D(f) \cap D(r)\) (functoriality of restriction through \(D(f)\)); the
2953	  localization datum at \(U = D(r)\) then provides \(k_r\) with
2954	  \(f^{k_r} \cdot x|_{D(r)} = 0\). Take \(n := \max_{r \in t} k_r\); then
2955	  \((f^n \cdot x)|_{D(r)} = f^{\,n-k_r} \cdot \bigl(f^{k_r} \cdot x|_{D(r)}\bigr) = 0\)
2956	  for every \(r\), and since the \(D(r)\) cover \(\Spec R\), sheaf separatedness
2957	  (\cref{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}) gives \(f^n \cdot x = 0\).
2958	
2959	  \emph{Surjectivity.} Let \(y \in \Gamma(M, D(f))\); we find a global \(x\) and \(n\)
2960	  with \(f^n \cdot y = x|_{D(f)}\). For each \(r \in t\), the localization datum at
2961	  \(U = D(r)\) writes \(f^{m_r} \cdot y|_{D(f) \cap D(r)}\) as the restriction of a
2962	  section \(b_r \in \Gamma(M, D(r))\); normalizing with the common exponent
2963	  \(N := \max_r m_r\) (replace \(b_r\) by \(f^{\,N-m_r} \cdot b_r\)) we may assume
2964	  \(b_r|_{D(f) \cap D(r)} = f^{N} \cdot y|_{D(f) \cap D(r)}\) for every \(r\). On a
2965	  pairwise overlap \(D(r) \cap D(r')\) — an open contained in \(D(r)\) — the difference
2966	  \(b_r - b_{r'}\) restricts to \(0\) on \(D(f) \cap D(r) \cap D(r')\), since both terms
2967	  restrict to \(f^N \cdot y\) there; the localization datum at
2968	  \(U = D(r) \cap D(r')\) provides a power \(f^{P_{r,r'}}\) killing the difference, and
2969	  taking \(P := \max P_{r,r'}\) over the finitely many pairs, the family
2970	  \(\{f^P \cdot b_r\}_{r \in t}\) agrees on all overlaps. By the sheaf axiom (unique
2971	  gluing over the finite cover \(\{D(r)\}_{r \in t}\) of \(\Spec R\)) the family glues to
2972	  a global section \(x \in \Gamma(M, \top)\). Finally
2973	  \(x|_{D(f)} = f^{N+P} \cdot y\): the two sides agree on each member of the cover
2974	  \(\{D(f) \cap D(r)\}_{r \in t}\) of \(D(f)\), so they are equal by separatedness
2975	  (\cref{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}).
2976	\end{proof}
2977	
2978	% --- (D, basic-open cover form) The form gap1 actually consumes ---

2981	  \label{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover}

2983	  \leanok

2990	  \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover} still holds: the global restriction
2991	  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is the localization at the powers of \(f\)
2992	  over \(R\).
2993	\end{lemma}

2996	  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover, lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
2997	  The proof of \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover} consults the
2998	  localization datum only at the cover members \(U = D(r)\) (\(r \in t\)) and at their
2999	  pairwise intersections \(U = D(r) \cap D(r') = D(r r')\) — all of them \emph{basic}

3034	  \(h : N_1 \to N_2\) be an \(A\)-linear map intertwining \(g\), i.e.
3035	  \(h \circ e_1 = e_2 \circ g\). Then \(h\), regarded as an \(R\)-linear map, exhibits
3036	  \(N_2\) as the localization of \(N_1\) at the powers of \(f\).
3037	\end{lemma}
3038	\begin{proof}
3039	  \leanok
3040	  We check the three localization conditions in two stages.
3041	
3042	  \emph{Stage I: crossing \(\sigma\).} The map \(h\) is a localization at the powers of
3043	  \(\sigma(f')\) over \(A\).
3044	  \emph{Units:} for \(k \ge 0\), the semilinearity of \(e_2\) gives that the action of
3045	  \(\sigma(f')^k = \sigma(f'^k)\) on \(N_2\) equals
3046	  \(e_2 \circ (\text{action of } f'^k \text{ on } M_2) \circ e_2^{-1}\), a composite of
3047	  bijections because \(f'^k\) acts invertibly on the localization \(M_2\).
3048	  \emph{Surjectivity:} given \(y \in N_2\), choose \(x \in M_1\) and \(k\) with
3049	  \(f'^k \cdot e_2^{-1}(y) = g(x)\); applying \(e_2\) and semilinearity,
3050	  \(\sigma(f')^k \cdot y = e_2(g(x)) = h(e_1(x))\).
3051	  \emph{Uniqueness:} if \(h(y_1) = h(y_2)\) then, by the intertwining relation,
3052	  \(e_2\bigl(g(e_1^{-1} y_1)\bigr) = e_2\bigl(g(e_1^{-1} y_2)\bigr)\), so
3053	  \(g(e_1^{-1} y_1) = g(e_1^{-1} y_2)\); hence
3054	  \(f'^k \cdot e_1^{-1} y_1 = f'^k \cdot e_1^{-1} y_2\) for some \(k\), and applying
3055	  \(e_1\), \(\sigma(f')^k \cdot y_1 = \sigma(f')^k \cdot y_2\).
3056	
3057	  \emph{Stage II: descending to \(R\).} By hypothesis \(\sigma(f')\) is the image of
3058	  \(f\) under \(R \to A\), and by compatibility of the two module structures the action
3059	  of \(f^k\) through \(R\) coincides with the action of the image of \(f^k\) through
3060	  \(A\), on \(N_1\) and on \(N_2\). Each of the three conditions at the powers of \(f\)
3061	  over \(R\) is therefore literally the corresponding Stage-I condition at the powers of
3062	  \(\sigma(f')\) over \(A\): the units condition transfers because the two actions
3063	  coincide; surjectivity reads \(f^k \cdot y = \sigma(f')^k \cdot y = h(x)\); and
3064	  uniqueness likewise.
3065	\end{proof}
3066	
3067	\begin{lemma}[Section-transport producer: basic-open localization inside a cover member
3068	(gap1, Hfr)]
3069	  \label{lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}
3070	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}
3071	  \leanok
3072	  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
3073	    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
3074	    lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport,
3075	    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}
3076	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) with quasi-coherence data (an open cover
3077	  \(\{X_i\}\) with per-member presentations, as in
3078	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}), let \(f, s \in R\), and
3079	  suppose \(D(s) \subseteq X_i\) for some \(i\). Then the section restriction
3080	  \[
3081	    \Gamma\bigl(M, D(s)\bigr) \longrightarrow \Gamma\bigl(M, D(f) \cap D(s)\bigr)
3082	  \]
3083	  exhibits the target as the localization of \(\Gamma(M, D(s))\) at the powers of \(f\),
3084	  over \(R\).
3085	\end{lemma}
3086	\begin{proof}
3087	  \leanok
3088	  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
3089	    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
3090	    lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport,
3091	    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}
3092	  Let \(W := \iota_{X_i}^{-1}(D(s))\) be the affine open of the subscheme \(Z := X_i\)
3093	  corresponding to \(D(s)\), put \(S := \Gamma(Z, W)\), and let
3094	  \(j : \Spec S \to \Spec R\) be the composite open immersion
3095	  \(\Spec S \cong W \hookrightarrow Z \hookrightarrow \Spec R\) (the affine
3096	  identification of \(W\) followed by the two canonical immersions). Its image is
3097	  \(D(s)\): the first leg is an isomorphism onto \(W\), and
3098	  \(\iota_{X_i}(W) = X_i \cap D(s) = D(s)\).
3099	
3100	  Write \(M' := j^{*} M\) (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}). The per-element
3101	  transport \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent} gives invertibility of
3102	  the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit for the iterated pullback of \(M\) along the three legs of
3103	  \(j\); pullback along a composite agrees with the composite of the pullbacks up to
3104	  canonical natural isomorphism, and invertibility of the counit is invariant under
3105	  isomorphism of modules (the essential image of the tilde functor is closed under
3106	  isomorphism). Hence the counit of \(M'\) is invertible, and by
3107	  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}, for every \(f' \in S\) the
3108	  restriction \(\Gamma(M', \top) \to \Gamma(M', D(f'))\) is the localization at the
3109	  powers of \(f'\) over \(S\).
3110	
3111	  Let \(\sigma : S \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Gamma(\Spec R, D(s))\) be the ring comparison
3112	  of the open immersion \(j\) on top sections
3113	  (\(S \cong \Gamma(\Spec S, \top) \cong \Gamma(\Spec R, j(\top))\), and
3114	  \(j(\top) = D(s)\)), and choose \(f' := \sigma^{-1}\bigl(f|_{D(s)}\bigr)\). The image
3115	  \(j\bigl(D(f')\bigr)\) is the locus of \(D(s)\) where \(\sigma(f') = f|_{D(s)}\) is
3116	  invertible, that is, \(j\bigl(D(f')\bigr) = D(f) \cap D(s)\).
3117	
3118	  Since \(j\) is an open immersion, applying the section functor to the identification of
3119	  the pullback with the restriction (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib})
3120	  gives additive isomorphisms
3121	  \(e_1 : \Gamma(M', \top) \cong \Gamma\bigl(M, D(s)\bigr)\) and
3122	  \(e_2 : \Gamma(M', D(f')) \cong \Gamma\bigl(M, D(f) \cap D(s)\bigr)\) which intertwine
3123	  the two restriction maps (naturality of the identification in the open) and are
3124	  \(\sigma\)-semilinear over the structure-sheaf comparisons: the action on the sections
3125	  of the pullback is through the immersion's section-ring isomorphism.
3126	
3127	  Finally apply the combiner \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_transport} with
3128	  \(A := \Gamma(\Spec R, D(s))\) — an \(R\)-algebra through restriction of global
3129	  sections — the isomorphism \(\sigma\), the elements \(f\) and \(f'\) (so that
3130	  \(\sigma(f')\) is the image of \(f\) in \(A\)), the localization
3131	  \(g := \bigl(\Gamma(M', \top) \to \Gamma(M', D(f'))\bigr)\), the comparisons
3132	  \(e_1, e_2\), and \(h := \bigl(\Gamma(M, D(s)) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \cap D(s))\bigr)\):
3133	  the conclusion is that \(h\) is the localization at the powers of \(f\) over \(R\).
3134	\end{proof}
3135	
3136	\begin{lemma}[Section-localization descent for quasi-coherent modules (gap1 keystone)]
3137	  \label{lem:section_localization_descent}
3138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent}
3139	  \leanok
3140	  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover,
3141	    lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}
3142	  For a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules \(M\) on \(\Spec R\) and \(f \in R\), the section
3143	  restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the
3144	  localization \(\Gamma(M, \top)[1/f]\) over \(R\). This is the statement of
3145	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} at the affine open \(\top\) of
3146	  \(\Spec R\), proved here by finite-cover descent --- not through the affine
3147	  equivalence of quasi-coherent modules with modules, which is instead \emph{derived}
3148	  from this lemma in \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}.
3149	\end{lemma}
3150	\begin{proof}
3151	  \leanok
3152	  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover,
3153	    lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen}
3154	  Quasi-coherence provides a cover \(\{X_i\}\) of \(\Spec R\) with per-member
3155	  presentations. Refine it to a finite basic-open cover: the basic opens form a basis of
3156	  \(\Spec R\), so every point has a basic-open neighbourhood contained in some \(X_i\);
3157	  quasi-compactness of \(\Spec R\) extracts a finite family \(t \subseteq R\) with each
3158	  \(D(r)\), \(r \in t\), contained in some \(X_i\), and covering is equivalent to \(t\)
3159	  generating the unit ideal. For every basic open \(D(u)\) contained in some \(D(r)\)
3160	  with \(r \in t\) — hence contained in a cover member \(X_i\) — the producer
3161	  \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_basicOpen} supplies the localization datum at
3162	  \(D(u)\). The basic-open cover form
3163	  \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_basicOpen_cover} then yields the conclusion.
3164	\end{proof}
3165	
3166	
3167	\subsection{G1-assemble: from per-basic-open section localization to the
3168	\texorpdfstring{$\widetilde{(-)}$}{tilde}-counit}
3169	
3170	This subsection records the bridge \emph{section localization on every basic open}
3171	\(\Rightarrow\) \emph{invertible tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit}: given that the section
3172	restriction localizes on every basic open, the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit is an
3173	isomorphism. Together with its converse
3174	\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ} it assembles the equivalence
3175	\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}, which is exactly what makes G1-core
3176	(\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}) and gap1
3177	(\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}) interderivable -- the route by which G1-core is obtained as
3178	a corollary of gap1. The supporting Mathlib facts enter through the tilde--\(\Gamma\)

3183	\begin{lemma}[Basic-open section localization for a module with invertible
3184	tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit]
3185	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
3186	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
3187	  \leanok
3188	  \uses{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}
3189	  % SOURCE: Stacks, Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-widetilde-pullback} and the remark
3190	  % following it (restricting a tilde to a standard open D(f) yields the tilde of the
3191	  % localized module; read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1242-L1274).
3192	  \textit{Source: Stacks, Schemes, Lemma \texttt{widetilde-pullback} (sections of a
3193	  tilde over a basic open are the localized module).}
3194	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) whose tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
3195	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(M, \top)} \to M\) (\cref{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}) is an
3196	  isomorphism. Then for every \(f \in R\) the section restriction
3197	  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the localization of
3198	  \(\Gamma(M, \top)\) at the powers of \(f\), over \(R\).
3199	\end{lemma}
3200	\begin{proof}
3201	  \leanok
3202	  \uses{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}
3203	  First let \(M = \widetilde{N}\) be a tilde. The unit
3204	  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top)\) of the adjunction
3205	  (\cref{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}) is an isomorphism, and the canonical map
3206	  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the localized module
3207	  \(N_f\) — the sections of a tilde over a basic open are the localization. The
3208	  canonical map factors as the unit followed by the section restriction, so the
3209	  restriction \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top) \to \Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) is the
3210	  composite of the inverse unit isomorphism with a localization map, hence itself a
3211	  localization at the powers of \(f\).
3212	
3213	  For general \(M\), put \(N := \Gamma(M, \top)\). The counit isomorphism
3214	  \(\widetilde{N} \cong M\) induces, naturally in the open, \(R\)-linear isomorphisms
3215	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top) \cong \Gamma(M, \top)\) and
3216	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f)) \cong \Gamma(M, D(f))\) intertwining the two restriction
3217	  maps. Pre- and post-composing a localization map with \(R\)-linear isomorphisms
3218	  compatible in this way preserves the localization property.
3219	\end{proof}
3220	
3221	\begin{lemma}[Characterization of the invertible tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit by
3222	section localization]
3223	  \label{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
3224	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
3225	  \leanok
3226	  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
3227	    lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}
3228	  For a sheaf of modules \(M\) on \(\Spec R\), the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
3229	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(M, \top)} \to M\) is an isomorphism if and only if for every
3230	  \(f \in R\) the section restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits
3231	  the target as the localization of \(\Gamma(M, \top)\) at the powers of \(f\) over
3232	  \(R\).
3233	\end{lemma}
3234	\begin{proof}
3235	  \leanok
3236	  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
3237	    lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}
3238	  The forward direction is \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}.
3239	
3240	  For the converse, write \(N := \Gamma(M, \top)\) and let
3241	  \(\varepsilon : \widetilde{N} \to M\) be the counit. Fix \(f \in R\). The
3242	  \(D(f)\)-component of \(\varepsilon\) is a map between two localizations of \(N\) at
3243	  the powers of \(f\): the source \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) receives the canonical
3244	  map from \(N\) (sections of a tilde over a basic open, as in the forward direction),
3245	  and the target \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\) receives the restriction
3246	  \(N = \Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\), a localization by hypothesis. The
3247	  component intertwines these two maps out of \(N\): the counit composed with the
3248	  canonical section map is the section restriction, by the compatibility of the counit
3249	  with the adjunction unit (\cref{lem:tilde_gamma_adjunction_mathlib}) and naturality.
3250	  A map between two localizations of the same module at the same multiplicative set
3251	  commuting with the two localization maps is the canonical isomorphism between them,
3252	  hence bijective. Thus \(\varepsilon\) is an isomorphism on every basic open.
3253	
3254	  Since the basic opens form a basis of \(\Spec R\), \(\varepsilon\) is an isomorphism
3255	  on every stalk: injective, because a germ in the kernel is represented by a section
3256	  over some basic neighbourhood killed by \(\varepsilon\) there, hence zero; surjective,
3257	  because every germ of \(M\) is the germ of a section over some basic open, which is
3258	  hit by the corresponding section of \(\widetilde{N}\). A morphism of sheaves that is
3259	  an isomorphism on all stalks is an isomorphism, and a morphism of sheaves of
3260	  modules whose underlying morphism of sheaves is an isomorphism is itself an
3261	  isomorphism.
3262	\end{proof}
3263	
3264	\begin{lemma}[A quasi-coherent module on an affine scheme is the tilde of its sections
3265	(gap1, Stacks 01I8)]
3266	  \label{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
3267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isQuasicoherent}
3268	  \leanok
3269	  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent,
3270	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}

3272	  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1278-L1340): a quasi-coherent module on Spec(R) is
3273	  % isomorphic to the sheaf associated to the R-module of its global sections.
3274	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01I8}{01I8}
3275	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}).}
3276	  For a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules \(M\) on \(\Spec R\), the tilde--\(\Gamma\)
3277	  counit \(\widetilde{\Gamma(M, \top)} \to M\) is an isomorphism; equivalently, \(M\)
3278	  lies in the essential image of the tilde functor, i.e. \(M\) is isomorphic to the
3279	  tilde of an \(R\)-module.

3284	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}

3286	  section restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is the localization at the

3289	\end{proof}

3292	  \label{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}

3298	  section restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the
3299	  localization \(\Gamma(M, \top)[1/f]\) over \(R\). This is the statement of
3300	  \cref{lem:section_localization_descent} in its clean named form — obtained here as a
3301	  corollary of gap1 rather than by re-running the descent — and the affine instance
3302	  (\(X = \Spec R\), \(U = \top\)) of the general-scheme keystone
3303	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
3304	\end{lemma}
3305	\begin{proof}
3306	  \leanok
3307	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
3308	    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
3309	  Gap1 (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}) makes the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit of
3310	  \(M\) an isomorphism, and
3311	  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ} applies.
3312	\end{proof}

3315	\begin{definition}
3316	
3317	\leanok
3318	[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]

text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/Axel/LeanAlgebraicGeometry-Horizon/.claude/skills/blueprint-conventions

Blueprints are a LaTeX subset (the `leanblueprint` dialect) parsed into the DAG.
Write timeless mathematics — a standalone document, not a project journal.

## Environments

Each declaration is an amsthm-style environment; the recognized ones are
`theorem`, `lemma`, `proposition`, `corollary`, `definition`, `remark`,
`example`, `notation`, `convention`, and `proof`. A statement environment plus
its following `proof` environment form one node's content.

## Per-node macros

- `\label{id}` — a stable id (the DAG node key).
- `\lean{Name}` — the Lean declaration this node formalizes. Keep it 1-to-1: one
  blueprint node ↔ one Lean declaration, signatures matching.
- `\uses{a,b}` — dependencies on other nodes' labels; these drive the DAG edges.
  Add an edge instead of re-explaining a dependency in prose. Put `\uses` in the
  statement for definitional deps and in the `proof` for deps used only in the
  proof.
- `\source{slug:page-0001}` — reference anchors for UI popups and audit trails.
  Use page-level ids from `references/<slug>/tex/page-0001.tex` or an equivalent
  manifest entry. Multiple anchors may be comma-separated.
- `\leanok` — the Lean is written and checked. Honest only when its Lean is
  actually checked *and* its dependencies are too. On a `proof`, it means the
  proof is formalized; on a statement, the signature is formalized.
- `\mathlibok` — the declaration is already available in mathlib (no
  formalization needed; treat as a leaf).

## House style

- **Pure mathematics only.** No Lean tactics, typeclass/implementation notes, or
  semi-Lean pseudocode. No project history ("since iter N", "our failed route")
  and no conversational filler. If a Lean helper exists with no math counterpart,
  write its LaTeX statement so the 1-to-1 correspondence holds.
- **Complete proofs, not sketches.** A node's `proof` is a real, rigorous
  mathematical proof a reader could check — not a hand-wave or a TODO. The way to
  keep it short is to *split*, not to abbreviate: if a proof is long or a node
  grows past a short paragraph, break out the hard step as its own `\uses`-linked
  lemma (down to sentence-sized lemmas if needed) so each node stays small and
  its proof stays complete.
- **One declaration's worth per node.** Don't restate Lean source as prose.
- **Cite by source link, not quote comments.** Add `\source{...}` to the
  relevant node. Keep the retrieved/transcribed page text in `references/` (for
  example `references/<slug>/tex/page-0042.tex`) and let the UI expose it; do not
  paste verbatim source quotes into `% QUOTE` comments inside the blueprint.

206	\section{Existence of the flattening stratification}
207	\label{sec:flatstrat_exists}
208	
209	\begin{theorem}
210	
211	\leanok
212	[Existence of the flattening stratification]
213	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
214	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification}
215	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat, lem:flat_locus_open, lem:nonflat_locus_proper,
216	    lem:noetherian_induction_strata, thm:generic_flatness}
217	
218	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification''
219	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1811-L1841).
220	  % Parallel pointer: [Stacks Project], tag 052H (verbatim text not yet
221	  % retrieved into a local references/ file; see ``Notes for Plan Agent'').
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
223	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, and let
224	  %  \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on the projective space \(\PP^n_S\)
225	  %  over \(S\). Then the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials of
226	  %  restrictions of \(\F\) to fibers of \(\PP^n_S\to S\)
227	  %  is a finite set. Moreover, for each \(f\in I\) there exist
228	  %  a locally closed subscheme \(S_f\) of \(S\),
229	  %  such that the following conditions are satisfied.
230	  %
231	  %  \textbf{(i) Point-set: } The underlying set \(|S_f|\) of \(S_f\) consists of
232	  %  all points \(s\in S\) where the Hilbert polynomial of the
233	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_s\) is \(f\). In particular,
234	  %  the subsets \(|S_f|\subset |S|\) are disjoint,
235	  %  and their set-theoretic union is \(|S|\).
236	  %
237	  %  \textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
238	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
239	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
240	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
241	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
242	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
243	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
244	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
245	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\).
246	  %
247	  %  \textbf{(iii) Closure of strata: }
248	  %  Let the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials be given a total
249	  %  ordering, defined by putting \(f<g\) whenever \(f(n) < g(n)\) for all
250	  %  \(n \gg  0\). Then the closure in \(S\) of the subset \(|S_f|\)
251	  %  is contained in the union of all \(|S_g|\) where \(f\le g\)."
252	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'';
253	  cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 052H.}
254	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(\F\) a coherent sheaf on the relative
255	  projective space \(\PP^n_S\) over \(S\).  Then:
256	  \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
257	    \item The set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials
258	      \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) that arise as \(s\) ranges over \(S\) is
259	      finite.
260	    \item For each \(f \in I\) there exists a locally-closed subscheme
261	      \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set \(|S_f|\) consists of exactly
262	      those \(s \in S\) with \(P_s = f\).  The collection \(\{|S_f|\}_{f \in I}\) is
263	      pairwise disjoint with set-theoretic union \(|S|\).  Forming the coproduct
264	      \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) and the morphism \(i : S' \to S\) assembled
265	      from the inclusions \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\), the pullback \(i^* \F\) on
266	      \(\PP^n_{S'}\) is flat over \(S'\), and \(i : S' \to S\) is universal with this
267	      property: for any morphism \(\varphi : T \to S\) such that \(\varphi^* \F\) on
268	      \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\), \(\varphi\) factors uniquely through \(i\).
269	      Each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).
270	    \item Order \(I\) by \(f < g \iff f(n) < g(n)\) for all \(n \gg 0\). Then the
271	      closure of \(|S_f|\) in \(|S|\) is contained in \(\bigcup_{g \ge f} |S_g|\).
272	  \end{enumerate}
273	\end{theorem}
274	
275	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "It is enough to prove the theorem for open subschemes of
276	% $S$ which cover $S$, as the resulting strata will then glue together by their
277	% universal property. [...] \textbf{General case: } We now allow the integer
278	% $n$ to be arbitrary. The idea of the proof is as follows: We show the
279	% existence of a stratification of $S$ which is a `g.c.d.' of the flattening
280	% stratifications for direct images $\pi_*\F(i)$ for all $i \ge N$ for some
281	% integer $N$ (where the flattening stratifications for $\pi_*\F(i)$ exist by
282	% case $n=0$ which we have treated above). This is the desired flattening
283	% stratification of $\F$ over $S$, as follows from Lemma \ref{graded module is flat}."
284	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1844-L1900; the
285	% full body L1844-L2090 is distributed across the sub-lemmas below, each of
286	% which carries its own verbatim % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF.)
287	\begin{proof}
288	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat, lem:flat_locus_open, lem:nonflat_locus_proper,
289	    lem:noetherian_induction_strata, thm:generic_flatness}
290	  Following [Nitsure], \S4, the construction reduces to the special case
291	  \(n = 0\), assembled over the relative twists \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\).  As the
292	  argument is local on \(S\) (the strata glue by their universal property, part
293	  (ii)), and as the three conclusions are proved through the sub-lemmas below,
294	  we assemble them in the order (i), (ii), (iii).
295	
296	  \emph{Part (i): finiteness of the Hilbert-polynomial set.}
297	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}, Noetherian induction on \(S\) (whose
298	  inductive step invokes generic flatness, Theorem~\ref{thm:generic_flatness},
299	  on the integral locally-closed strata cut out by the irreducible-component
300	  decomposition of \(S\)) produces finitely many reduced, pairwise disjoint,
301	  locally-closed subschemes \(V_i \hookrightarrow S\) set-theoretically covering
302	  \(|S|\) on each of which \(\F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}\) is flat over \(\OO_{V_i}\) in
303	  the sense of Definition~\ref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}.  On a flat family the
304	  Hilbert polynomial \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is locally constant, so
305	  it takes finitely many values on each quasi-compact \(V_i\); the finite union
306	  over the finitely many \(V_i\) shows that the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials
307	  occurring on \(S\) is finite.
308	
309	  \emph{Part (ii): existence of the strata and their universal property.}
310	  Working over the flat pieces \(V_i\) of part (i), semicontinuity
311	  (cohomology-and-base-change) yields a uniform integer \(N\) such that, for all
312	  \(s \in S\) and \(m \ge N\), the higher cohomologies \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\)
313	  vanish for \(r \ge 1\) and the base-change homomorphism
314	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism.  With this
315	  \(N\) fixed, Lemma~\ref{lem:noetherian_induction_strata} applies the \(n = 0\)
316	  stratification mechanism (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}) successively to the
317	  coherent sheaves \(E_i = \pi_* \F(N+i)\) on \(S\) for \(i = 0, \dots, n\),
318	  refining stratum by stratum.  After \(n+1\) steps this produces a finite
319	  family of locally-closed subschemes \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n} \subseteq S\),
320	  universal for the property that each \(E_i\) pulls back to a locally-free sheaf
321	  of constant rank \(e_i\); re-indexing the tuple \((e_0, \dots, e_n)\) by the
322	  unique numerical polynomial \(f\) of degree \(\le n\) with \(f(N+i) = e_i\)
323	  gives the family \(\{W_f\}_{f \in I}\).  At any \(s \in W_f\) the base-change
324	  isomorphism and the cohomology vanishing force \(P_s = f\) (since \(P_s\) has
325	  degree \(\le n\) and is determined by its values at \(N, \dots, N+n\)), so
326	  \(|W_f| = \{s : P_s = f\}\); these subsets are disjoint with union \(|S|\).
327	  The required locally-closed subscheme \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) is the closed
328	  subscheme cut out by the further local-freeness conditions
329	  ``\(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) is locally free of rank \(f(N+i)\) for all
330	  \(i \ge 0\)'' (a chain of coherent ideal sheaves that stabilises by
331	  Noetherianity), and \(\F|_{\PP^n_{S_f}}\) is flat over \(S_f\)
332	  (Definition~\ref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}) because the graded module
333	  \(\bigoplus_{i \gg 0} \pi_* \F(i)|_{S_f}\) is locally free.  Forming
334	  \(S' = \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) and the assembled morphism \(i : S' \to S\),
335	  the pullback \(i^* \F\) is \(S'\)-flat; and any \(\varphi : T \to S\) with
336	  \(\varphi^* \F\) flat over \(T\) has locally constant Hilbert polynomial, hence
337	  decomposes \(T = \coprod_f T_f\) into the open-closed loci where the polynomial
338	  is \(f\), each factoring uniquely through the corresponding \(S_f\) by the
339	  local-freeness universal property of Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}.  This
340	  gives the unique factorisation through \(i\), and shows each \(S_f\) is
341	  uniquely determined by \(f\).
342	
343	  \emph{Part (iii): closure of strata.}
344	  Order \(I\) by \(f < g \iff f(n) < g(n)\) for all \(n \gg 0\).  Since each
345	  \(S_f\) is a stratum of the \(n = 0\) flattening stratification applied to a
346	  single direct image \(\pi_* \F(p)\) for \(p \gg 0\) separating all polynomials
347	  in \(I\), the closure-of-strata assertion reduces to the upper-semicontinuity
348	  statement already established in the \(n = 0\) case
349	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}): the closure of \(|S_f|\) in \(|S|\) is
350	  contained in \(\bigcup_{g \ge f} |S_g|\).
351	\end{proof}
352	
353	The proof is long; we split it into sub-lemmas below.  The first sub-lemma is
354	the special case \(n = 0\) (when \(\PP^n_S = S\) and \(\F\) is just a coherent sheaf
355	on \(S\)), which already contains the flat-locus-as-locally-closed-subscheme
356	mechanism. The remaining inputs are (A) the Noetherian-induction reduction to
357	the flat case via generic flatness; (B) uniform vanishing of higher cohomology
358	of \(\F(m)\) for \(m \gg 0\) on \(\F_{V_i}\) for the flat pieces; and (C) base-change
359	isomorphisms for the direct images \(\pi_* \F(m)\), which allow comparing
360	strata for different twists.
361	
362	\begin{lemma}
363	[Flat locus on \(S\) for a coherent sheaf is a locally-closed stratification]
364	  \label{lem:flat_locus_open}
365	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.flatLocusOpen}
366	
367	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Special case
368	  % \(n=0\) (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
369	  % L1849-L1885).
370	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

611	\subsection{Lean-side encodings of the sub-lemmas}
612	\label{sec:flatstrat_lean_helpers}
613	
614	The three sub-lemmas above are realised in Lean by the following helper
615	declarations, which carry the substantive type signatures consumed by the
616	existence theorem. Each is an internal project helper with no external source
617	beyond Nitsure~\S4 already cited above.
618	
619	\begin{lemma}
620	
621	\leanok
622	[Flat-locus stratification, special case \(n = 0\)]
623	  \label{lem:flat_locus_stratification_lean}
624	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusStratification}
625	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
626	  Let \(S\) be a locally noetherian scheme and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_S\)-module.
627	  Then there is a family of immersions \(\iota_e : S_e \to S\) indexed by
628	  \(e \in \mathbb{N}\) whose images are pairwise disjoint and set-theoretically
629	  cover \(|S|\), and such that for each \(e\) the pullback of \(\F\) along
630	  \(\iota_e\) is flat over \(S_e\) in the sense of
631	  \cref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}.  This is the Lean encoding of the special
632	  case \(n = 0\) of the flattening stratification
633	  (\cref{lem:flat_locus_open}), the rank index \(e\) playing the role of
634	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\).
635	\end{lemma}
636

638	  Proved directly in Lean.
639	\end{proof}

642	
643	\leanok

646	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusReduction}

649	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exist finitely many
650	  immersions \(\iota_i : V_i \to S\) with pairwise disjoint images
651	  set-theoretically covering \(|S|\), such that for each \(i\) the pullback of
652	  \(\F\) to \(X \times_S V_i\) is flat over \(V_i\) in the sense of
653	  \cref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}.  This is the Lean encoding of the
654	  Noetherian-induction reduction (\cref{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}), obtained by
655	  peeling off non-empty flat open patches via generic flatness
656	  (\cref{thm:generic_flatness}).
657	\end{lemma}

661	\end{proof}

663	\begin{lemma}
664	
665	\leanok
666	[Assembly of the Hilbert-polynomial-indexed strata]
667	  \label{lem:flat_locus_assembly_lean}
668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusAssembly}
669	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat, lem:flat_locus_stratification_lean,
670	    lem:flat_locus_reduction_lean}
671	  Let \(S\) be a locally noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a proper morphism,
672	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exist a finite index set
673	  \(I\), immersions \(\iota_f : S_f \to S\) (\(f \in I\)) with pairwise disjoint
674	  images set-theoretically covering \(|S|\), and a label map
675	  \(P : I \to (\mathbb{N} \to \Z)\) with \(f = g \iff P_f = P_g\), such that for
676	  each \(f\) the pullback of \(\F\) to \(X \times_S S_f\) is flat over \(S_f\) in
677	  the sense of \cref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}.  This is the Lean encoding of the
678	  assembly step (\cref{lem:noetherian_induction_strata}): the special-case
679	  stratification (\cref{lem:flat_locus_stratification_lean}) is iterated on the
680	  direct images \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) to refine the reduction
681	  (\cref{lem:flat_locus_reduction_lean}) into the Hilbert-polynomial-indexed
682	  family, with \(P\) recording the numerical-polynomial label of each stratum.
683	\end{lemma}

703	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

706	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
707	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
708	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
709	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on

718	  \(i : S' \to S\) assembled from the locally-closed inclusions.  Then \(i^* \F\)
719	  is flat over \(S'\), and the pair \((S', i)\) is universal: for any morphism
720	  \(\varphi : T \to S\) of noetherian schemes (one may take \(T\) arbitrary, the
721	  Noetherian hypothesis on \(S\) being preserved), the pullback
722	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is \(T\)-flat if and only if \(\varphi\) factors
723	  uniquely through \(i\). In particular each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).

730	% the base-change

732	% each \(S_f\). Therefore

734	% as the direct images \(\pi_*\F(i)\) for all \(i \ge N'\) are locally
735	% free over \(S_f\). Conversely, if \(\phi : T\to S\) is a morphism such
736	% that \(\F_T\) is flat, then the Hilbert polynomial is locally
737	% constant over \(T\). Let \(T_f\) be the open and closed subscheme of \(T\)
738	% where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\). Clearly, the set
739	% map \(|T_f| \to |S|\) factors via \(|S_f|\). But as the direct images
740	% \({\pi_T}_*\F_T(i)\) are locally free of rank \(f(i)\) on \(T_f\), it
741	% follows in fact that the schematic morphism \(T_f \to S\) factors

748	  base-change-without-flatness lemma plus the graded-module-flatness criterion

750	  Conversely, if \(\varphi : T \to S\) satisfies \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is
751	  \(T\)-flat, then \(T\)-flatness of the family forces the Hilbert polynomial to be
752	  locally constant on \(T\).  Decompose \(T = \coprod_f T_f\) into the open-closed

759	  morphism \(T_f \to S_f\) extending the set map. Assembling over \(f\) gives the

761	\end{proof}

input.old_string:
The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
non-zero element of the base ring.

\begin{theorem}
[Generic flatness, algebraic form]

\centerline{\large\bf Lemma on Generic Flatness}

\medskip


\begin{lemma} \label{algebraic lemma for generic flatness}
Let $A$ be a noetherian domain, 
and $B$ a finite type $A$ algebra. Let $M$ be a finite $B$-module. 
Then there exists an $f\in A$, $f\ne 0$, such that 
the localisation $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$.
\end{lemma}


\proof Over the quotient field $K$ of $A$, the $K$-algebra 
$B_K = K\otimes _AB$ is of finite type, and $M_K = K\otimes_AM$ is a 
finite module over $B_K$. Let $n$ be the dimension of the support
of $M_K$ over $\spec B_K$. We argue by induction on $n$, starting
with $n=-1$ which is the case when $M_K =0$. In this case, as
$K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$, each $v\in M$
is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating 
elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$.
Hence $M_f =0$, proving the lemma when $n=-1$.

Now let $n\ge 0$, and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
As $B$ is noetherian and $M$ is assumed to be a finite $B$-module,
there exists a finite filtration
$$0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M$$
where each $M_i$ is a $B$-submodule of $M$ such that for each $i\ge 1$
the quotient module $M_i/M_{i-1}$ is isomorphic to $B/\pp_i$ for 
some prime ideal $\pp_i$ in $B$. 

Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact
sequence of $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of
$A$ such that $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over
respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$, then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$
where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact repeatedly.
Therefore it is enough to prove the result when 
$M$ is of the form $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. 
This reduces us to the case where $B$ is a domain and $M=B$.

As by assumption $K\otimes_AB$ has dimension $n\ge 0$ (that is,
$K\otimes_AB$ is non-zero), the map $A\to B$ must be injective.
By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements 
$b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$, 
such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra
$K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and 
the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent
over $K$. (For simplicity of notation, we write $1\otimes b$ simply as $b$.)
If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for  
coefficients of equations of integral dependence 
satisfied by a finite set of
algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, 
we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. 

Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
$$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$.
Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$), 
there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$.
Taking $f=gh$, the lemma follows from the 
above short exact sequence.
\hfill$\square$



The above theorem has the following consequence, which follows
by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of $S$.



\begin{theorem}\label{generic flatness} 
Let $S$ be a noetherian and integral scheme.
Let $p: X\to S$ be a finite type morphism, and let $\F$ be a 
coherent sheaf of ${\mathcal O}_X$-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
open subscheme $U\subset S$ such that the restriction of $\F$

\begin{theorem}
[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
% there exists a finite filtration
% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
\begin{proof}
  
  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
  finite \(B\)-module
  \[
    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
  \]
  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent
  \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\) with \(B_K\) finite over \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); choosing
  \(g \ne 0\) to clear denominators of integrality equations, \(B_g\) is finite over
  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Let \(m\) be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
  \[
    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
  \]
  has \(T\) a finite torsion module over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so $\dim
  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
  yields \(h \ne 0\) in \(A\) with \(T_h\) free over \(A_{gh}\); set \(f := gh\).
\end{proof}

288	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
289	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
290	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.

\begin{theorem}
[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
% there exists a finite filtration
% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
\begin{proof}
  
  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
  finite \(B\)-module
  \[
    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
  \]
  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent
  \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\) with \(B_K\) finite over \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); choosing
  \(g \ne 0\) to clear denominators of integrality equations, \(B_g\) is finite over
  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Let \(m\) be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
  \[
    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
  \]
  has \(T\) a finite torsion module over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so $\dim
  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
  yields \(h \ne 0\) in \(A\) with \(T_h\) free over \(A_{gh}\); set \(f := gh\).
\end{proof}

input.new_string:
\textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
  Let \(S\) be an integral locally noetherian scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a
  finite-type morphism (that is, quasi-compact and locally of finite type —
  quasi-compactness is essential: it bounds the number of denominators to
  clear, and the statement fails for the locally-of-finite-type morphism
  \(\coprod_q \Spec \mathbb{F}_q \to \Spec \Z\)), and \(\F\) a coherent
  \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open subscheme
  \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
\end{theorem}
